user_input: What is this project?
retrieved_contexts:
["Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The problem it solves: recruiters reading a CV cannot tell verified fact from self-promotion, cannot quickly gauge how strong a candidate actually is in a given skill, and cannot interrogate a static document. This system turns a pile of documents into an interactive, grounded, honest representative that cites real evidence, refuses to answer what it does not know, and quantifies skill levels with a trained model instead of keyword guessing.

The whole system can run on either a 100% local open-source stack or an all-API cloud stack, selected purely through configuration with no code changes. The cloud configuration is what makes it deployable as an always-on, secured, low-cost service; the local configuration is what made it possible to build, train, and evaluate everything for free on the developer's own hardware.", 'Section: STRUCTURED STORE AND VECTOR STORE
The structured store is a small JSON file holding the verified profile fields plus, per listed skill, the supporting evidence found in the documents — but not the model's numeric score, which is candidate-private and never written to disk. It is the source for get_structured_data and get_skill_proficiency, both of which therefore answer instantly and deterministically.

The vector store is ChromaDB, used as a persistent on-disk index with cosine similarity. Each candidate's chunks live in their own collection, with a paired "_summaries" collection for that candidate's summary index. This project overview lives in its own separate collection ("project_kb", plus its summary collection), which is what the search_project tool queries — keeping project knowledge cleanly isolated from candidate data so the two can never bleed into each other's retrieval.', "Section: DEPLOYMENT AND SECURITY
Several security properties are enforced by design. In production only the recruiter chat is served and the setup page is hidden, so recruiters can neither edit the profile nor trigger ingestion. The candidate's private data (the vector database and the structured profile) is kept out of any public repository. API keys live only in environment variables or managed secrets and are never committed. This very project-overview document is intentionally free of private information and secrets.

Running costs are minimal. The all-API serve path uses a fast, inexpensive Claude model for the agent and router, and a low-cost Voyage tier for embeddings and reranking — typically only a few dollars per month of light use — and the hosting itself can be free. The expensive work (training the scorer, generating synthetic data, parsing documents, running the full evaluation) all happens locally at build time and adds nothing to the hosting bill.", 'Section: CANDIDATE AI AGENT — COMPLETE PROJECT OVERVIEW
This document is the knowledge base for questions about the software project / system itself: what it is, how it is built, every major flow, and the engineering decisions behind it. It also describes how this AI agent itself works, so that when a recruiter asks the agent second-person questions ("how do you work?", "what reranker do you use?", "how were you built?", "what model are you?"), the agent can answer about its own architecture from this document. It contains no private candidate data, no personal information, and no API keys or secrets.', 'Section: CANDIDATE AI AGENT — COMPLETE PROJECT OVERVIEW
A note on "you" and "I": throughout this document, when the agent speaks about how "I" work or what "I" use, it is describing this very system. Questions about how the agent/assistant operates, is built, retrieves information, scores skills, was evaluated, or is deployed are all answered from this document. (In contrast, "your name", "your skills", "your experience" refer to the candidate's professional profile, which lives in the candidate's own documents and structured store, not here.)', 'Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled (skill, evidence, level) data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a full synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents containing controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage across generations to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the corpus with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection analysis — the latter to ensure the model learns from genuine evidence rather than spurious surface cues (such as a level always co-occurring with a particular phrase). Generation uses asynchronous LLM calls for throughput. The result is a labeled dataset large and clean enough to fine-tune an ordinal scorer that generalizes to real documents.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
search_project runs the same RAG pipeline, but over this project-overview document instead of the candidate's data, in a separate vector collection. It answers questions about the system itself and about how the agent works — architecture, technology stack, the RAG pipeline, the skill scorer, the evaluation suite, deployment, and design decisions — including second-person questions addressed to the agent ("how do you work?", "what model are you?", "what reranker do you use?"). This is the tool serving the very answer you are reading.

The system prompt instructs the agent to distinguish the two RAG tools by subject: a question about the candidate goes to search_documents; a question about the software project or how the system/agent works goes to search_project. It also tells the agent never to guess, to keep answers concise and professional, to refuse out-of-scope questions, and to answer in the recruiter's language.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
get_skill_proficiency returns the supporting evidence found in the candidate's documents for a named skill — the exact passages a retrieval model surfaced for it at setup time. With no skill named it lists every assessed skill. This is the tool for any "how good / how strong / how proficient / how skilled / how experienced" question, or to characterize skills. It intentionally does NOT return a numeric rating: the trained model's 1-to-5 score is candidate-private (see below), so the agent describes the evidence qualitatively rather than quoting a level. If a requested skill was not assessed, the tool says so and the agent falls back to document search.

This project is a system designed to help recruiters evaluate candidates by transforming their uploaded documents (e.g., CVs, certificates) into an interactive, evidence-based representation of their skills and experience. Here's how it works:

1. **Problem Solved**:

This system quantifies a candidate's skill proficiency using a trained model, citing specific evidence from their documents (e.g., projects, responsibilities) rather than relying on keywords alone.

response:
The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered system designed to provide recruiters with an interactive, evidence-based representative of a candidate's qualifications. Here's a high-level overview:

1. **Core Functionality**:  
   - Transforms static candidate documents (CVs, certificates, etc.) into a dynamic, verified profile that distinguishes factual evidence from self-promotion.  
   - Quantifies skill proficiency using a fine-tuned NLP model and machine-learning scorer, avoiding keyword-based assumptions.  

2. **Architecture**:  
   - **Agentic ReAct Framework**: Combines tool-calling with reasoning for structured, context-aware responses.  
   - **Multi-Stage RAG Pipeline**: Retrieves, contextualizes, and generates answers grounded in the candidate’s data (via semantic search and document evidence).  

3. **Deployment Options**:  
   - **Local Setup**: Open-source, infrastructure-free for development and training.  
   - **Cloud API**: Low-cost, always-on deployment for real-time interaction.  

4. **Workflow Split**:  
   - **Build Phase**: Processes documents, scores skills, and prepares data.  
   - **Serve Phase**: Handles real-time queries from recruiters.  

5. **Evaluation**:  
   - Rigorous multi-dimensional testing ensures accuracy and reliability.  

This system streamlines hiring by prioritizing verified evidence and quantifiable expertise, reducing reliance on subjective CV interpretations.
reference: An agentic recruiter-facing chat that routes between verified structured facts, evidence from a trained skill-proficiency scorer, document RAG, and project RAG. It has a build phase (local ingestion, embedding, and skill scoring) and an always-on serve phase (retrieval plus LLM calls).

user_input: How does the skill proficiency estimation work?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
Rather than leaving "how good is the candidate at X?" to keyword matching, the project trains a dedicated NLP model to score each skill from 1 to 5 directly from the candidate's own documents. The scale is ordinal and defined as: 1 = awareness (a passing mention), 2 = working familiarity, 3 = competent / day-to-day use, 4 = strong / leads work in it, 5 = expert / authority.', "Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
At setup time, for each skill the candidate lists, the system retrieves that skill's supporting evidence chunks from the candidate's documents (routing and summary are off here, because a bare skill name is always a specific query) and runs the trained scorer. The 1-to-5 result is shown to the candidate right there in the setup page as private feedback — a visual, ranked view that tells them which skills are well-supported and which need more evidence — so they can strengthen a weak profile before sharing it. Only the evidence chunks are written into the structured store; the numeric score is deliberately never persisted and never exposed to recruiters. The recruiter chat then answers skill questions from that curated evidence instantly via get_skill_proficiency — grounded in real passages but without quoting a score — instead of recomputing or guessing at query time.", 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
get_skill_proficiency returns the supporting evidence found in the candidate's documents for a named skill — the exact passages a retrieval model surfaced for it at setup time. With no skill named it lists every assessed skill. This is the tool for any "how good / how strong / how proficient / how skilled / how experienced" question, or to characterize skills. It intentionally does NOT return a numeric rating: the trained model's 1-to-5 score is candidate-private (see below), so the agent describes the evidence qualitatively rather than quoting a level. If a requested skill was not assessed, the tool says so and the agent falls back to document search.

search_documents runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other two tools: projects, detailed experience, what they did with a technology, achievements, certifications, and richer context. It is also the fallback when a skill was not among the assessed proficiencies.', 'Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
The scorer itself is a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head. CORAL is used because the 1-to-5 levels are ordered, not arbitrary categories — confusing a 4 with a 5 is a smaller error than confusing a 1 with a 5, and an ordinal head encodes that structure. The model reads the skill name plus its evidence chunks, serialized in exactly the format it saw in training ("{skill} {sep} {chunk1} {chunk2} ..."). The best-performing configuration fine-tunes the top transformer layers; head-only and plain-softmax classifier variants were also trained and compared, and the ordinal CORAL configuration won. Because the scorer (and document parsing) only run at build time, neither is ever deployed to the always-on service.', 'Section: THE TWO USER FLOWS AND PAGES
The application is a multi-page Streamlit app with two pages.

The Candidate Setup page is for the candidate only. Here the candidate uploads supporting documents (CV, transcripts, certificates, recommendation letters) in PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD, PNG, or JPG form; fills in structured profile fields (contact details, education, experience, job preferences); lists the skills they want assessed; and triggers skill-proficiency estimation and ingestion. This page can edit the profile and run the heavy pipeline, so in production it is hidden entirely — a recruiter can never reach it, edit the profile, or trigger ingestion.', "Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The single most important architectural decision is the split between a build phase and a serve phase.

The build phase runs locally and only occasionally. The candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, performs section-aware chunking, embeds the chunks into a vector store, generates per-document summaries, and runs the trained scoring model to score each listed skill. This produces two persistent artifacts: a ChromaDB vector index (the unstructured knowledge) and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus, for each listed skill, the supporting evidence found in the documents. The model's 1-to-5 score itself is shown to the candidate as private setup feedback and is deliberately not persisted (see the skill-proficiency section). All heavyweight machine learning — document parsing, OCR, the DeBERTa skill scorer, and optionally a local embedder and reranker — only ever runs in this phase, on the candidate's own machine.", 'Section: STRUCTURED STORE AND VECTOR STORE
The structured store is a small JSON file holding the verified profile fields plus, per listed skill, the supporting evidence found in the documents — but not the model's numeric score, which is candidate-private and never written to disk. It is the source for get_structured_data and get_skill_proficiency, both of which therefore answer instantly and deterministically.

The vector store is ChromaDB, used as a persistent on-disk index with cosine similarity. Each candidate's chunks live in their own collection, with a paired "_summaries" collection for that candidate's summary index. This project overview lives in its own separate collection ("project_kb", plus its summary collection), which is what the search_project tool queries — keeping project knowledge cleanly isolated from candidate data so the two can never bleed into each other's retrieval.', "Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The problem it solves: recruiters reading a CV cannot tell verified fact from self-promotion, cannot quickly gauge how strong a candidate actually is in a given skill, and cannot interrogate a static document. This system turns a pile of documents into an interactive, grounded, honest representative that cites real evidence, refuses to answer what it does not know, and quantifies skill levels with a trained model instead of keyword guessing.

The whole system can run on either a 100% local open-source stack or an all-API cloud stack, selected purely through configuration with no code changes. The cloud configuration is what makes it deployable as an always-on, secured, low-cost service; the local configuration is what made it possible to build, train, and evaluate everything for free on the developer's own hardware."]
response:
The skill proficiency estimation works through a trained NLP model that analyzes a candidate's uploaded documents (CVs, certificates, etc.) to assign a 1–5 score to each listed skill. Here's how it functions:

1. **Model & Scoring**:  
   - A fine-tuned **DeBERTa-v3** transformer model with a **CORAL ordinal regression head** is used. This handles the ordered nature of the 1–5 scale (e.g., confusing a 4 with 5 is less critical than confusing a 1 with 5).  
   - The model evaluates **skill name + evidence chunks** (excerpts from documents) to determine proficiency levels:  
     1 = Awareness (passing mention), 2 = Familiarity, 3 = Competent, 4 = Strong expertise, 5 = Expert/authority.

2. **Evidence-Based Feedback**:  
   - During candidate setup, the system retrieves and scores evidence chunks for each skill. The numeric score (1–5) is shown as **private feedback** to the candidate but **never persisted** or shared with recruiters.  
   - Only the **evidence chunks** (passages from documents) are saved in a structured store, enabling tools like `get_skill_proficiency` to answer skill questions by citing specific document excerpts.

3. **Build vs. Serve Phases**:  
   - **Build phase**: Runs locally when the candidate uploads documents. It parses, chunks, embeds, and scores skills using the model. Heavy ML (e.g., OCR, embedding) occurs here.  
   - **Serve phase**: The system operates efficiently using preprocessed data (vector store + structured store), with no real-time model inference.

4. **Recruiter Interaction**:  
   - Recruiters access skill evidence via `get_skill_proficiency` or `search_documents`, which surface grounded passages from the candidate’s documents without exposing the numeric score.  

This approach ensures transparency, avoids keyword guessing, and provides recruiters with verifiable, evidence-based insights into a candidate’s skills.
reference: A fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head scores each listed skill from 1 to 5 using the evidence chunks retrieved from the candidate's documents. The score is shown to the candidate as private setup feedback, and only the supporting evidence is saved into the structured store; it runs only at setup time.

The project uses the following evaluation frameworks and methodologies:

1. **RAGAS** - Measures RAG quality with metrics like faithfulness, context precision, context recall, and answer relevancy.
2. **DeepEval (GEval)** - Acts as an LLM "judge" to assess answer correctness and appropriateness, including proper refusal wording.

response: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) is used in the retrieval pipeline to merge results from multiple search methods (dense vector search and sparse keyword search) into a unified ranked list. It scores each individual ranked list independently by assigning higher values to top-ranked items, regardless of the list's length or origin. This approach combines keyword-based precision (from BM25 Okapi) and semantic similarity (from ChromaDB embeddings) without requiring calibration between their raw scores. The fused list then serves as the input for the cross-encoder reranker, enhancing recall while preserving diverse retrieval signals.
reference: RRF merges the ranked result lists from dense vector search and BM25 keyword search (across the expanded query variations) into a single fused ranking, scoring each list independently so the top of any list gets equal weight.

user_input: How are documents ingested?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
Documents are parsed with the Unstructured library, backed by PyMuPDF for PDFs, python-docx for Word files, and OCR via Tesseract and Pillow for images. The parser performs section-aware extraction rather than dumping flat text. It detects section headers — including unusual spaced-letter headers like "T E C H N I C A L  S K I L L S" via a dedicated regular expression — and merges consecutive elements under the same heading into one section block. It is careful not to mistake content for headers: "Title" elements that contain numbers, emails, or telephone markers (for example "GPA: 94.3" or a contact line) are kept as content, not promoted to section names.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
Alongside the per-chunk index, a separate summary index is built. For each document the LLM writes a short factual summary, which is embedded and stored in its own ChromaDB collection. This summary index powers the "broad" retrieval path described below. There is also a lighter in-memory ingestion path for text that is already plain (no file parsing, no summary) — used, for example, when the skill-scorer training corpus is ingested verbatim so the model sees exact text.', 'Section: THE TWO USER FLOWS AND PAGES
The application is a multi-page Streamlit app with two pages.

The Candidate Setup page is for the candidate only. Here the candidate uploads supporting documents (CV, transcripts, certificates, recommendation letters) in PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD, PNG, or JPG form; fills in structured profile fields (contact details, education, experience, job preferences); lists the skills they want assessed; and triggers skill-proficiency estimation and ingestion. This page can edit the profile and run the heavy pipeline, so in production it is hidden entirely — a recruiter can never reach it, edit the profile, or trigger ingestion.', "Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The single most important architectural decision is the split between a build phase and a serve phase.

The build phase runs locally and only occasionally. The candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, performs section-aware chunking, embeds the chunks into a vector store, generates per-document summaries, and runs the trained scoring model to score each listed skill. This produces two persistent artifacts: a ChromaDB vector index (the unstructured knowledge) and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus, for each listed skill, the supporting evidence found in the documents. The model's 1-to-5 score itself is shown to the candidate as private setup feedback and is deliberately not persisted (see the skill-proficiency section). All heavyweight machine learning — document parsing, OCR, the DeBERTa skill scorer, and optionally a local embedder and reranker — only ever runs in this phase, on the candidate's own machine.", 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — INGESTION
Each section is then split with a recursive character text splitter (about 1000 characters per chunk with 150 characters of overlap, preferring to break on paragraph, line, sentence, then word boundaries). Every chunk is prefixed with its section name for retrieval context ("Section: <name>") and stored with provenance metadata: the source file and a document id. This provenance is essential — it lets any retrieved chunk be traced back to its origin document, which is what makes retrieval evaluation against ground-truth evidence possible.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
A deliberate design touch: the pipeline exposes the fused candidate pool from before re-ranking. This lets the evaluation suite localize exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion (it was never indexed), at recall (it never entered the fused pool), or at re-ranking (it entered the pool but was cut from the final top-k) — turning a single aggregate recall number into an actionable, per-stage diagnosis.

The embeddings are asymmetric: documents and queries are embedded with different prefixes ("search_document:" versus "search_query:"), which matches how the embedding models were trained and improves retrieval quality. Because the stored document vectors bake in the document prefix, switching the embedding provider requires rebuilding the vector index.', 'Section: TECHNOLOGY STACK
Frontend and app framework: Streamlit, multi-page. LLM and orchestration: Anthropic Claude API (or local Ollama), ReAct-style tool calling, prompt caching, a provider-neutral client. Embeddings: Voyage AI (or local nomic-embed via sentence-transformers). Retrieval: ChromaDB for dense vectors, BM25 Okapi for sparse search, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and a cross-encoder reranker (Voyage rerank or Qwen3-Reranker). Skill scoring: DeBERTa-v3 with a CORAL ordinal head, in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Ingestion: Unstructured, PyMuPDF, python-docx, Tesseract OCR, and section-aware recursive chunking via LangChain text splitters. Evaluation: RAGAS and DeepEval (GEval), plus custom tool, router, refusal, and per-gate retrieval evaluators, with HTML, JSON, and CSV reports. Configuration: a central settings module resolving environment variables, then Streamlit secrets, then defaults.', "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?

Per-gate Retrieval Analysis: for specific document questions with known evidence, it localizes exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion, at recall, or at re-ranking — using the pre-rerank fused pool the pipeline exposes.

Ingestion Quality: chunk statistics, coverage, and summary quality.

The harness seeds each candidate's data into a fresh collection, runs the pipeline over that candidate's questions, then scores every component on the combined result set and generates an HTML report (with companion JSON and CSV) showing both per-candidate and aggregate breakdowns. It supports checkpointing and resuming, so a long run can be interrupted and continued. Because the LLM judges can themselves run on a free local model, a full evaluation can cost nothing to run."]
response:
Documents are ingested through a structured pipeline with the following key steps:

1. **Parsing**: 
   - Files (PDF, DOCX, TXT, images) are parsed using `Unstructured` with backend tools: 
     - `PyMuPDF` for PDFs, `python-docx` for Word files, and OCR via `Tesseract`/`Pillow` for images.
   - Section-aware extraction identifies headers (even spaced ones like "T E C H N I C A L  S K I L L S") and merges content under the same heading. Content mistakenly labeled as headers (e.g., "GPA: 94.3") remains as content.

2. **Chunking**:
   - Text is split into chunks using a recursive character splitter (≈1000 chars/chunk, 150 overlap), prioritizing paragraph/sentence boundaries.
   - Each chunk is prefixed with its section name (e.g., "Section: EDUCATION") and stored with metadata (source file, document ID).

3. **Summary Index**:
   - An LLM generates a factual summary for each document, embedded and stored in a separate ChromaDB collection for "broad" retrieval.

4. **Build Phase**:
   - Ingestion runs locally during the candidate's setup, parsing documents, embedding chunks, and generating skill proficiency scores. 
   - Heavy ML (OCR, embedding, skill scoring) occurs here, producing two artifacts: 
     - A vector index (for dense retrieval) and 
     - A structured JSON store of verified facts and skill evidence.

5. **Provenance**:
   - Metadata traces each chunk back to its origin document, enabling precise retrieval evaluation.

This process ensures structured, searchable storage while preserving document context and enabling rigorous evaluation.
reference: They are parsed with the Unstructured library (PyMuPDF for PDFs, python-docx for Word, Tesseract OCR for images) using section-aware extraction, then split into roughly 1000-character overlapping chunks prefixed with their section name and stored with source provenance. A per-document summary index is also built.

A safety cap (five rounds) prevents infinite tool loops; if it is hit, the loop does one final pass with tools disabled, forcing the model to produce text.", 'Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered digital representative for a single job candidate. A recruiter opens a secured, always-available chat link and asks natural-language questions about the candidate; the agent answers accurately and professionally, grounded strictly in the candidate's own documents and a trained machine-learning scoring model. It is deliberately more than a standard "chat with your PDF" demo: it combines an agentic ReAct-style tool-calling architecture, an advanced multi-stage Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline, and a purpose-built, fine-tuned NLP model for skill-proficiency scoring, all wrapped in a rigorous multi-dimensional evaluation suite.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS

This is the tool serving the very answer you are reading.

The system prompt instructs the agent to distinguish the two RAG tools by subject: a question about the candidate goes to search_documents; a question about the software project or how the system/agent works goes to search_project. It also tells the agent never to guess, to keep answers concise and professional, to refuse out-of-scope questions, and to answer in the recruiter's language.', "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
The project ships a rigorous, component-based evaluation harness rather than ad-hoc spot checks. It runs the full agent over golden datasets and scores several independent dimensions.

Tool Selection: did the agent pick the correct tool — structured facts versus skill scorer versus candidate document search versus project search — for each question?

RAG Quality: RAGAS metrics (faithfulness, context precision, context recall, and answer relevancy) computed on the RAG-routed questions, using the retrieved contexts captured during the run.

Answer Correctness: DeepEval's GEval LLM-as-judge on every question, scoring whether the final answer is correct and appropriate, including whether refusals are worded correctly.

Refusal Accuracy: a confusion matrix checking that the agent refuses out-of-scope or inappropriate questions and does not refuse legitimate ones.

Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?", 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
get_skill_proficiency returns the supporting evidence found in the candidate's documents for a named skill — the exact passages a retrieval model surfaced for it at setup time. With no skill named it lists every assessed skill. This is the tool for any "how good / how strong / how proficient / how skilled / how experienced" question, or to characterize skills. It intentionally does NOT return a numeric rating: the trained model's 1-to-5 score is candidate-private (see below), so the agent describes the evidence qualitatively rather than quoting a level. If a requested skill was not assessed, the tool says so and the agent falls back to document search.

search_documents runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other two tools: projects, detailed experience, what they did with a technology, achievements, certifications, and richer context. It is also the fallback when a skill was not among the assessed proficiencies.', 'Section: NEUTRAL LLM CLIENT
Every LLM interaction in the project — the agent loop, the broad/specific query router, query expansion, and the build-time document summaries — goes through a single provider-neutral client. The client exposes two methods: complete() for simple prompt-to-text calls, and chat() for tool-calling conversations. The standout engineering choices are: a trained, evidence-grounded skill scorer instead of keyword guessing; an agent that routes between verified facts, the scorer, candidate document search, and project search with intelligent fallbacks; a hybrid, fused, re-ranked retrieval pipeline fronted by a broad/specific router and query expansion; a serious multi-dimensional evaluation suite, including the per-gate retrieval failure localization that pinpoints whether recall or re-ranking lost a chunk; a fully synthetic, validated training-data pipeline built because the needed labels did not exist; and a clean build-versus-serve split with three pluggable provider layers that lets one codebase run entirely on free local open-source models or deploy as a cheap, secured, always-on cloud service', 'Section: CANDIDATE AI AGENT — COMPLETE PROJECT OVERVIEW
This document is the knowledge base for questions about the software project / system itself: what it is, how it is built, every major flow, and the engineering decisions behind it. It also describes how this AI agent itself works, so that when a recruiter asks the agent second-person questions ("how do you work?", "what reranker do you use?", "how were you built?", "what model are you?"), the agent can answer about its own architecture from this document. It contains no private candidate data, no personal information, and no API keys or secrets.']
response:
The agent has four core tools, each serving a distinct purpose in answering recruiter questions about the candidate or the system itself:

1. **`get_structured_data`**  
   Retrieves fixed, verified candidate information (e.g., contact details, education summary, job preferences) from a structured database. Used for straightforward, factual queries.

2. **`get_skill_proficiency`**  
   Provides evidence from the candidate’s documents (e.g., project descriptions, certifications) to characterize their skills or proficiency in specific technologies. Does **not** return numeric ratings but instead highlights relevant passages demonstrating expertise.

3. **`search_documents`**  
   Executes a semantic search over the candidate’s unstructured documents (CVs, project details, etc.) for richer context, detailed experience, or specifics about projects, achievements, or technical work.

4. **`search_project`**  
   Answers questions about the system itself (e.g., how the agent works, its architecture, or the RAG pipeline) or second-person queries about the agent’s operation (e.g., "How do you retrieve documents?"). Uses a separate vector database for project-overview documentation.

These tools are governed by a ReAct-style loop with intelligent fallbacks, ensuring answers are grounded in evidence while avoiding speculation. The system also includes a rigorous evaluation suite to validate tool selection and response accuracy.
reference: Four tools: get_structured_data for verified facts, get_skill_proficiency for the document evidence behind a skill, search_documents for RAG over the candidate's documents, and search_project for RAG over the project's own documentation.

response:
The **build-vs-serve split** is a core architectural design that separates heavy computational tasks from the hosted, always-on service. Here's how it works:

### **Build Phase**  
- **Local & Occasional**: Runs on the candidate's machine during setup.  
- **Responsibilities**:  
  - Parses and processes uploaded documents (e.g., CVs, certificates).  
  - Performs chunking, embedding, and generates summaries.  
  - Trains a skill-scoring model (e.g., DeBERTa) to assess proficiency.  
  - Stores results in a vector database (e.g., ChromaDB) and a structured JSON file (verified facts + skill evidence).  
- **Heavy ML Work**: Includes document parsing, OCR, embedding, and model training.  

### **Serve Phase**  
- **Hosted & Always-On**: A lightweight, scalable chat interface (e.g., Streamlit) for recruiters.  
- **Responsibilities**:  
  - Retrieves precomputed artifacts (vector DB, structured data) for queries.  
  - Uses lightweight LLMs (e.g., Claude) for routing and responses.  
  - Avoids heavy models to minimize hosting costs and complexity.  

### **Key Benefits**  
- **Cost Efficiency**: Expensive ML work (training, parsing) happens locally; the hosted service remains minimal.  
- **Security**: Private data (vector DB, structured profile) is isolated from public repositories.  
- **Flexibility**: Supports deployment as a free local tool or a cheap cloud service.  

This split enables a sophisticated system to be both powerful and affordable.
reference: The build phase runs locally and parses documents, embeds them, and runs the skill scorer to produce the vector index and structured store. The serve phase is hosted and always on and only performs retrieval and LLM calls, avoiding any heavy local model so it fits cheap hosting.

user_input: What do the 1 to 5 skill proficiency levels mean?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
Rather than leaving "how good is the candidate at X?" to keyword matching, the project trains a dedicated NLP model to score each skill from 1 to 5 directly from the candidate's own documents. The scale is ordinal and defined as: 1 = awareness (a passing mention), 2 = working familiarity, 3 = competent / day-to-day use, 4 = strong / leads work in it, 5 = expert / authority.', "Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
At setup time, for each skill the candidate lists, the system retrieves that skill's supporting evidence chunks from the candidate's documents (routing and summary are off here, because a bare skill name is always a specific query) and runs the trained scorer. The 1-to-5 result is shown to the candidate right there in the setup page as private feedback — a visual, ranked view that tells them which skills are well-supported and which need more evidence — so they can strengthen a weak profile before sharing it. Only the evidence chunks are written into the structured store; the numeric score is deliberately never persisted and never exposed to recruiters. The recruiter chat then answers skill questions from that curated evidence instantly via get_skill_proficiency — grounded in real passages but without quoting a score — instead of recomputing or guessing at query time.", 'Section: SKILL PROFICIENCY ESTIMATION — THE FLAGSHIP FEATURE
The scorer itself is a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 transformer with a CORAL ordinal regression head. CORAL is used because the 1-to-5 levels are ordered, not arbitrary categories — confusing a 4 with a 5 is a smaller error than confusing a 1 with a 5, and an ordinal head encodes that structure. The model reads the skill name plus its evidence chunks, serialized in exactly the format it saw in training ("{skill} {sep} {chunk1} {chunk2} ..."). The best-performing configuration fine-tunes the top transformer layers; head-only and plain-softmax classifier variants were also trained and compared, and the ordinal CORAL configuration won. Because the scorer (and document parsing) only run at build time, neither is ever deployed to the always-on service.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS
get_skill_proficiency returns the supporting evidence found in the candidate's documents for a named skill — the exact passages a retrieval model surfaced for it at setup time. With no skill named it lists every assessed skill. This is the tool for any "how good / how strong / how proficient / how skilled / how experienced" question, or to characterize skills. It intentionally does NOT return a numeric rating: the trained model's 1-to-5 score is candidate-private (see below), so the agent describes the evidence qualitatively rather than quoting a level. If a requested skill was not assessed, the tool says so and the agent falls back to document search.

search_documents runs the full RAG pipeline over the candidate's unstructured documents for anything not covered by the other two tools: projects, detailed experience, what they did with a technology, achievements, certifications, and richer context. It is also the fallback when a skill was not among the assessed proficiencies.', "Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The single most important architectural decision is the split between a build phase and a serve phase.

The build phase runs locally and only occasionally. The candidate uploads documents; the system parses them, performs section-aware chunking, embeds the chunks into a vector store, generates per-document summaries, and runs the trained scoring model to score each listed skill. This produces two persistent artifacts: a ChromaDB vector index (the unstructured knowledge) and a small JSON structured store of verified facts plus, for each listed skill, the supporting evidence found in the documents. The model's 1-to-5 score itself is shown to the candidate as private setup feedback and is deliberately not persisted (see the skill-proficiency section). All heavyweight machine learning — document parsing, OCR, the DeBERTa skill scorer, and optionally a local embedder and reranker — only ever runs in this phase, on the candidate's own machine.", 'Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled (skill, evidence, level) data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a full synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents containing controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage across generations to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the corpus with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection analysis — the latter to ensure the model learns from genuine evidence rather than spurious surface cues (such as a level always co-occurring with a particular phrase). Generation uses asynchronous LLM calls for throughput. The result is a labeled dataset large and clean enough to fine-tune an ordinal scorer that generalizes to real documents.', 'Section: THE EVALUATION DATA
Evaluation uses six synthetic candidates with deliberately varied document formats — PDF, DOCX, TXT, Markdown, and image/PNG specifically to exercise the OCR path. Each candidate has a golden dataset of around 75 questions spanning personal facts, education, experience, preferences, skills, skill proficiency, projects, certifications, complex multi-tool questions, and deliberately out-of-scope "negative" questions that the agent should refuse. A separate project-knowledge dataset asks questions about this system itself; those project questions are routed to search_project and evaluated the same way as the candidate document questions, so adding the new tool did not require a new scoring path. The pre-existing candidate evaluation data was left unchanged when the project dataset and tool were added.', 'Section: THE TWO USER FLOWS AND PAGES
The application is a multi-page Streamlit app with two pages.

The Candidate Setup page is for the candidate only. Here the candidate uploads supporting documents (CV, transcripts, certificates, recommendation letters) in PDF, DOCX, TXT, MD, PNG, or JPG form; fills in structured profile fields (contact details, education, experience, job preferences); lists the skills they want assessed; and triggers skill-proficiency estimation and ingestion. This page can edit the profile and run the heavy pipeline, so in production it is hidden entirely — a recruiter can never reach it, edit the profile, or trigger ingestion.']
response:
The 1 to 5 skill proficiency levels are an **ordinal scale** used to quantify a candidate's expertise in a specific skill, based on evidence from their uploaded documents. Here's what each level means:

- **1 = Awareness**: A passing mention or basic recognition of the skill (e.g., "mentioned in a project summary").  
- **2 = Working Familiarity**: Some hands-on experience or routine use (e.g., "used Python for data analysis in a side project").  
- **3 = Competent / Day-to-Day Use**: Proficient in standard tasks (e.g., "developed REST APIs using Node.js").  
- **4 = Strong / Leads Work**: Advanced skills with leadership or specialized applications (e.g., "architected a microservices system with Kafka").  
- **5 = Expert / Authority**: Demonstrated mastery, often through mentorship, publications, or high-impact projects (e.g., "published research on NLP frameworks").  

### Key Notes:
- **Scores are private**: The numeric rating (1–5) is shown to candidates during setup as feedback but is **never persisted or shared** with recruiters.  
- **Evidence-driven**: Recruiters access **qualitative evidence** (specific document excerpts) via `get_skill_proficiency`, not the raw score.  
- **Model-based**: The system uses a fine-tuned NLP model (DeBERTa-v3 with CORAL ordinal regression) to analyze document evidence and assign levels.  

This approach ensures transparency while protecting candidate privacy.
reference: 1 is awareness (a passing mention), 2 is working familiarity, 3 is competent or day-to-day use, 4 is strong or leads work in it, and 5 is expert or authority.

response:
The training data for the skill scorer was created using a **synthetic data generation system** since no public labeled datasets existed for this purpose. Here's how it was done:

1. **Realistic Personas & Documents**: The system generated synthetic candidate personas and archetypes, along with documents containing controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels (1–5).  
2. **Controlled Evidence**: Skill evidence was embedded into documents in a way that mirrored real-world scenarios, ensuring diverse and realistic usage patterns.  
3. **Avoiding Repetition**: Phrase usage across generations was tracked to prevent redundancy, and outputs were sanitized for consistency.  
4. **Validation**: The corpus underwent statistical checks, cross-validation, and **shortcut-detection analysis** to ensure the model learned from genuine evidence (not surface-level correlations like fixed phrase-level associations).  
5. **Asynchronous LLM Generation**: Generation was done via asynchronous LLM calls to maximize throughput.  

This synthetic dataset was then used to fine-tune the ordinal skill scorer (a DeBERTa-v3 model with a CORAL regression head), enabling it to generalize to real candidate documents.
reference: It was synthetically generated: realistic candidate personas and archetypes with controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, phrase tracking to avoid repetition, sanitization, and validation via statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut detection.

user_input: Which LLM does the project use?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: PLUGGABLE PROVIDERS
Three layers are pluggable purely through configuration, with no code changes, and this is what lets the same codebase run fully local or fully cloud.

The LLM layer is either Claude via the Anthropic API (production default) or a local Ollama model. All LLM uses — agent, router, query expansion, build summaries — go through the one provider-neutral client described above, with prompt caching on the Anthropic path.

The embeddings layer is either the Voyage AI API (production default, no local model) or a local nomic-embed-text model via sentence-transformers. Both are asymmetric document/query embedders.', 'Section: NEUTRAL LLM CLIENT
Every LLM interaction in the project — the agent loop, the broad/specific query router, query expansion, and the build-time document summaries — goes through a single provider-neutral client. The client exposes two methods: complete() for simple prompt-to-text calls, and chat() for tool-calling conversations. It returns a normalized Reply object carrying the assistant text and any tool calls in a provider-independent shape.', 'Section: SYNTHETIC TRAINING DATA GENERATION
Training the skill scorer required labeled (skill, evidence, level) data that does not exist publicly, so the project includes a full synthetic data generation system. It builds realistic candidate personas and archetypes, generates documents containing controlled skill evidence at known proficiency levels, tracks phrase usage across generations to avoid repetition, sanitizes the output, and validates the corpus with statistical checks, cross-validation, and shortcut-detection analysis — the latter to ensure the model learns from genuine evidence rather than spurious surface cues (such as a level always co-occurring with a particular phrase). Generation uses asynchronous LLM calls for throughput. The result is a labeled dataset large and clean enough to fine-tune an ordinal scorer that generalizes to real documents.', "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
The project ships a rigorous, component-based evaluation harness rather than ad-hoc spot checks. It runs the full agent over golden datasets and scores several independent dimensions.

Tool Selection: did the agent pick the correct tool — structured facts versus skill scorer versus candidate document search versus project search — for each question?

RAG Quality: RAGAS metrics (faithfulness, context precision, context recall, and answer relevancy) computed on the RAG-routed questions, using the retrieved contexts captured during the run.

Answer Correctness: DeepEval's GEval LLM-as-judge on every question, scoring whether the final answer is correct and appropriate, including whether refusals are worded correctly.

Refusal Accuracy: a confusion matrix checking that the agent refuses out-of-scope or inappropriate questions and does not refuse legitimate ones.

Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?", 'Section: TECHNOLOGY STACK
Frontend and app framework: Streamlit, multi-page. LLM and orchestration: Anthropic Claude API (or local Ollama), ReAct-style tool calling, prompt caching, a provider-neutral client. Embeddings: Voyage AI (or local nomic-embed via sentence-transformers). Retrieval: ChromaDB for dense vectors, BM25 Okapi for sparse search, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and a cross-encoder reranker (Voyage rerank or Qwen3-Reranker). Skill scoring: DeBERTa-v3 with a CORAL ordinal head, in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Ingestion: Unstructured, PyMuPDF, python-docx, Tesseract OCR, and section-aware recursive chunking via LangChain text splitters. Evaluation: RAGAS and DeepEval (GEval), plus custom tool, router, refusal, and per-gate retrieval evaluators, with HTML, JSON, and CSV reports. Configuration: a central settings module resolving environment variables, then Streamlit secrets, then defaults.', 'Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The serve phase is the hosted, always-on recruiter chat. It only reads the prebuilt artifacts and performs retrieval plus LLM calls. It deliberately contains no heavy local model, so the serve image is small (no PyTorch, no transformers, no Unstructured) and fits free or near-free hosting tiers. This separation is what makes the project simultaneously sophisticated to build and cheap and simple to host. The lean serve dependencies are listed in requirements.txt; the full build/eval/training stack is in requirements-dev.txt.', 'Section: GPU MEMORY MANAGEMENT DURING SCORING
When the local stack is used, the DeBERTa scorer and the local Ollama LLM contend for the same GPU. The DeBERTa model with disentangled attention is memory-heavy, and a resident Ollama model with multiple parallel slots can occupy most of the card. To run both on a modest GPU, the build phase time-slices the device: just before the scoring burst it asks the Ollama server to evict its model from VRAM, runs the scorer, and then unloads the scorer and clears the CUDA cache so Ollama can reload (honoring its parallelism) for the question-answering work. The batch size of the scorer is also tunable via an environment variable. None of this applies to the API stack, where no local model is resident.', "Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING — THE ReAct LOOP
The system does not blindly query a vector database for every question. Instead an LLM agent reasons about each question and chooses among four tools, and it can chain multiple tools within a single turn, including intelligent fallbacks.

The agent loop works as follows. The recruiter's message is appended to the conversation history. A per-turn working copy of the messages is sent to the LLM together with the system prompt and the four tool schemas. If the model responds with no tool calls, that response is the final answer. If the model requests one or more tools, the loop records the assistant's tool-call turn, executes every requested tool, feeds each tool result back as a tool message, and loops again — so the model can now either call another tool (for example, fall back to document search) or produce a final answer. A safety cap (five rounds) prevents infinite tool loops; if it is hit, the loop does one final pass with tools disabled, forcing the model to produce text."]
response: The project uses either **Claude via the Anthropic API** (production default) or a **local Ollama model** for its LLM layer. This is configured through the pluggable providers system, allowing seamless switching between cloud and local execution without code changes. The same provider-neutral client handles all LLM interactions, including the agent loop, query routing, and document summarization.
reference: Claude via the Anthropic API by default, or a local Ollama model; the choice is configurable, and the same provider-neutral client serves the agent loop, the router, query expansion, and build-time summaries.

response:
The **per-gate retrieval analysis** is a diagnostic component of the evaluation suite designed to identify exactly *where* a relevant chunk of information was lost during the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline. It breaks down retrieval failures into three distinct stages:

1. **Ingestion**: The chunk was never indexed (e.g., due to parsing errors, formatting issues, or exclusion during chunking).  
2. **Recall**: The chunk entered the fused retrieval pool but was excluded during the hybrid fusion stage (BM25 + vector search + RRF).  
3. **Re-ranking**: The chunk was included in the fused pool but was dropped during the final cross-encoder reranking step.  

This analysis transforms aggregate recall metrics into actionable insights by isolating stage-specific failures. For example, if a query’s ground-truth evidence is missing from the response, the analysis determines whether the issue stems from incomplete ingestion, poor recall, or suboptimal re-ranking.  

The process relies on the pipeline’s design to expose the **fused candidate pool** before re-ranking, enabling precise localization. This is critical for refining the system’s retrieval strategies, such as improving chunking rules, tuning embeddings, or optimizing rerankers.  

The evaluation suite generates HTML/CSV reports with per-candidate and aggregate breakdowns, supporting iterative improvements to the RAG pipeline.
reference: For specific document questions it localizes where a relevant chunk was lost in retrieval — at ingestion, at recall (never entered the fused pool), or at re-ranking (cut from the final top-k) — instead of reporting only an aggregate recall number.

user_input: How do you decide which tool to call?
retrieved_contexts:
["Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING — THE ReAct LOOP
The system does not blindly query a vector database for every question. Instead an LLM agent reasons about each question and chooses among four tools, and it can chain multiple tools within a single turn, including intelligent fallbacks.

The agent loop works as follows. The recruiter's message is appended to the conversation history. A per-turn working copy of the messages is sent to the LLM together with the system prompt and the four tool schemas. If the model responds with no tool calls, that response is the final answer. If the model requests one or more tools, the loop records the assistant's tool-call turn, executes every requested tool, feeds each tool result back as a tool message, and loops again — so the model can now either call another tool (for example, fall back to document search) or produce a final answer. A safety cap (five rounds) prevents infinite tool loops; if it is hit, the loop does one final pass with tools disabled, forcing the model to produce text.", 'Section: TECHNOLOGY STACK

Embeddings: Voyage AI (or local nomic-embed via sentence-transformers). Retrieval: ChromaDB for dense vectors, BM25 Okapi for sparse search, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and a cross-encoder reranker (Voyage rerank or Qwen3-Reranker). Skill scoring: DeBERTa-v3 with a CORAL ordinal head, in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Ingestion: Unstructured, PyMuPDF, python-docx, Tesseract OCR, and section-aware recursive chunking via LangChain text splitters. Evaluation: RAGAS and DeepEval (GEval), plus custom tool, router, refusal, and per-gate retrieval evaluators, with HTML, JSON, and CSV reports. Configuration: a central settings module resolving environment variables, then Streamlit secrets, then defaults.', 'Section: AGENTIC TOOL CALLING — THE ReAct LOOP
Only the final assistant text is persisted into the conversation history; the intermediate tool-use / tool-result plumbing lives in a per-turn working copy. This keeps the stored history simple and serializable for Streamlit session state. The loop also records a tool trajectory (which tools were called, with what arguments, and a preview of each result) — this trajectory is exactly what the evaluation suite inspects to judge tool selection.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS

Answer Correctness: DeepEval's GEval LLM-as-judge on every question, scoring whether the final answer is correct and appropriate, including whether refusals are worded correctly.

The structured store is a small JSON file holding the verified profile fields plus, per listed skill, the supporting evidence found in the documents — but not the model's numeric score, which is candidate-private and never written to disk. This project overview lives in its own separate collection ("project_kb", plus its summary collection), which is what the search_project tool queries — keeping project knowledge cleanly isolated from candidate data so the two can never bleed into each other's retrieval.', 'Section: THE FOUR TOOLS

It is also the fallback when a skill was not among the assessed proficiencies.']
response:
The decision process follows the **ReAct loop** (Reason + Act), where the agent reasons about the question and selects the most appropriate tool from four options. Here's how it works:

1. **Question Analysis**: The agent examines the recruiter's query to determine its subject. If the question relates to the **candidate's profile** (e.g., skills, experience, projects), it routes to `search_documents` or `get_skill_proficiency`. If it concerns **this system's architecture, tools, or how the agent operates**, it uses `search_project`.

2. **Tool Selection**: 
   - `get_structured_data` is used for verified fields like contact info or education.
   - `get_skill_proficiency` provides evidence for specific skills (e.g., "Python" proficiency) from the candidate's documents.
   - `search_documents` handles detailed queries about the candidate's work, certifications, or projects.
   - `search_project` answers questions about the system's design, RAG pipeline, or how the agent itself functions.

3. **Fallbacks and Safety**: If a tool's results are incomplete, the agent may chain multiple tools (e.g., `get_skill_proficiency` followed by `search_documents`). A **safety cap** (5 rounds) prevents infinite loops; if exceeded, the agent produces a final answer without further tool calls.

4. **System Prompt Guidance**: The agent strictly adheres to rules (no guessing, concise answers, refusal of out-of-scope questions) and matches the recruiter's language. 

This process ensures accurate, context-aware responses while isolating candidate data from system knowledge in separate vector stores.
reference: An LLM agent reasons about each question in a ReAct-style loop and picks among four tools — get_structured_data for verified facts, get_skill_proficiency for the document evidence behind a skill, search_documents for RAG over the candidate's documents, and search_project for questions about the system itself. It routes by subject and can chain tools, for example falling back to document search if a skill was not assessed.

The reranker layer is either the Voyage rerank API (production default), a local Qwen3-Reranker, or "none" to skip reranking. The Qwen3 reranker is notable: it is a causal language model, not a classification cross-encoder, and is scored — per its model card — by reading the probability of the "yes" token at the final position of a query/document relevance prompt, rather than with a naive classification head.

The local backends are constructed lazily, so importing the embedder or reranker modules in the production (API) configuration never imports PyTorch or transformers. It contains no private candidate data, no personal information, and no API keys or secrets.', "Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
Third, hybrid fusion retrieval. For every query variation the system runs both dense vector search (semantic similarity over embeddings in ChromaDB) and sparse keyword search (BM25 Okapi) over the chunk corpus. All the resulting ranked lists are merged with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF). RRF scores each list independently by reciprocal rank, so a top result in any single list is rewarded regardless of that list's length or origin; this fuses keyword and semantic signals without having to calibrate their raw scores against each other.

Fourth, re-ranking. A cross-encoder reranker rescores the fused candidate pool against the original query and keeps the best top-k chunks, which become the context handed to the agent.", 'Section: DESIGN PRINCIPLES AND WHAT MAKES IT NOTABLE
The standout engineering choices are: a trained, evidence-grounded skill scorer instead of keyword guessing; an agent that routes between verified facts, the scorer, candidate document search, and project search with intelligent fallbacks; a hybrid, fused, re-ranked retrieval pipeline fronted by a broad/specific router and query expansion; a serious multi-dimensional evaluation suite, including the per-gate retrieval failure localization that pinpoints whether recall or re-ranking lost a chunk; a fully synthetic, validated training-data pipeline built because the needed labels did not exist; and a clean build-versus-serve split with three pluggable provider layers that lets one codebase run entirely on free local open-source models or deploy as a cheap, secured, always-on cloud service', 'Section: TECHNOLOGY STACK
Frontend and app framework: Streamlit, multi-page. LLM and orchestration: Anthropic Claude API (or local Ollama), ReAct-style tool calling, prompt caching, a provider-neutral client. Embeddings: Voyage AI (or local nomic-embed via sentence-transformers). Retrieval: ChromaDB for dense vectors, BM25 Okapi for sparse search, Reciprocal Rank Fusion, and a cross-encoder reranker (Voyage rerank or Qwen3-Reranker). Skill scoring: DeBERTa-v3 with a CORAL ordinal head, in PyTorch and Hugging Face Transformers. Ingestion: Unstructured, PyMuPDF, python-docx, Tesseract OCR, and section-aware recursive chunking via LangChain text splitters. Evaluation: RAGAS and DeepEval (GEval), plus custom tool, router, refusal, and per-gate retrieval evaluators, with HTML, JSON, and CSV reports. Configuration: a central settings module resolving environment variables, then Streamlit secrets, then defaults.', 'Section: THE RAG PIPELINE — RETRIEVAL
A deliberate design touch: the pipeline exposes the fused candidate pool from before re-ranking. This lets the evaluation suite localize exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion (it was never indexed), at recall (it never entered the fused pool), or at re-ranking (it entered the pool but was cut from the final top-k) — turning a single aggregate recall number into an actionable, per-stage diagnosis.

The embeddings are asymmetric: documents and queries are embedded with different prefixes ("search_document:" versus "search_query:"), which matches how the embedding models were trained and improves retrieval quality. Because the stored document vectors bake in the document prefix, switching the embedding provider requires rebuilding the vector index.', "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?

Per-gate Retrieval Analysis: for specific document questions with known evidence, it localizes exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion, at recall, or at re-ranking — using the pre-rerank fused pool the pipeline exposes.

Ingestion Quality: chunk statistics, coverage, and summary quality.

The harness seeds each candidate's data into a fresh collection, runs the pipeline over that candidate's questions, then scores every component on the combined result set and generates an HTML report (with companion JSON and CSV) showing both per-candidate and aggregate breakdowns. It supports checkpointing and resuming, so a long run can be interrupted and continued. Because the LLM judges can themselves run on a free local model, a full evaluation can cost nothing to run."]
response:
The reranker used depends on the configuration:  
- **Production default**: Voyage rerank API  
- **Local option**: Qwen3-Reranker (a causal language model that scores relevance by predicting the probability of the "yes" token in a relevance prompt).  

The system also supports skipping reranking entirely ("none"). The Qwen3 reranker is notable for its architecture and scoring mechanism, as detailed in the documentation.
reference: A cross-encoder reranker — the Voyage rerank API by default, or a local Qwen3-Reranker scored by the probability of the 'yes' token over a query/document relevance prompt, with a 'none' option that skips reranking.

response:
The Candidate AI Agent retrieves information from a candidate's documents using a **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** pipeline tailored to the query's context. Here's how it works:

1. **Structured Data**: For verified fields (e.g., contact info, education, job titles), it pulls data from a structured JSON store. This is instant and deterministic, using `get_structured_data`.

2. **Skill Proficiency**: For questions about a candidate's expertise in a specific skill (e.g., "How good is she at Python?"), it uses `get_skill_proficiency`. This tool surfaces **curated evidence** from the candidate's documents (e.g., project descriptions, responsibilities) that directly support the skill. It does **not** return numeric ratings but instead describes the quality and scope of the evidence (e.g., "mentioned across multiple projects" or "limited to a single mention").

3. **Document Search**: For detailed projects, experiences, or context not captured by structured data or skill-specific evidence, it uses `search_documents`. This runs a semantic search over the candidate's unstructured documents (CVs, certificates, etc.) using the vector store (ChromaDB) and hybrid retrieval techniques (BM25 + vector fusion). It also incorporates query expansion and reranking to ensure relevant passages are surfaced.

4. **Fallback Logic**: If a skill isn't assessed or the query requires deeper context, the system falls back to `search_documents` for richer, unstructured insights.

The process ensures answers are **grounded in the candidate's own documents**, avoiding assumptions or guesses. For questions about *how the system works* (like this one), the `search_project` tool provides transparency into the architecture and retrieval logic.
reference: Through a multi-stage RAG pipeline: an LLM router labels the query broad or specific; specific queries are expanded into keyword and natural-language variants; both dense vector search over ChromaDB and BM25 keyword search run and are merged with Reciprocal Rank Fusion; then a cross-encoder reranker keeps the best top-k chunks as context.

response:
The system was built with a clear separation between two phases: **build** and **serve**.  

1. **Build Phase**:  
   - Runs locally and occasionally. The candidate uploads documents, which are then parsed, split into chunks, embedded into a vector store (ChromaDB), and summarized.  
   - A trained skill-scoring model evaluates proficiency in listed skills, generating evidence-based scores and structured facts stored in a JSON file.  
   - Heavyweight tasks like document parsing, embedding, and scoring occur here, using open-source models or local infrastructure.  

2. **Serve Phase**:  
   - A lightweight, hosted chat interface that only reads prebuilt artifacts (vector store and structured data).  
   - Designed for efficiency, it avoids local models (no PyTorch, transformers, or Unstructured) to fit free/hosting tiers.  
   - Uses pluggable layers (e.g., Voyage reranker, Qwen3-Reranker) for flexible, scalable deployment.  

This design balances sophistication with simplicity, enabling robust functionality while keeping hosting costs low. The system prioritizes grounding in evidence, verifiability, and modular architecture for flexibility.
reference: As a multi-page Streamlit app with an agentic ReAct tool-calling loop over four tools, an advanced hybrid RAG pipeline, and a fine-tuned DeBERTa-v3 skill scorer. It has a local build phase (ingestion, embedding, skill scoring) and an always-on serve phase that only does retrieval and LLM calls, with pluggable local or cloud providers.

user_input: Can you run entirely on free, local models without any paid APIs?
retrieved_contexts:
['Section: PLUGGABLE PROVIDERS
Three layers are pluggable purely through configuration, with no code changes, and this is what lets the same codebase run fully local or fully cloud.

The LLM layer is either Claude via the Anthropic API (production default) or a local Ollama model. All LLM uses — agent, router, query expansion, build summaries — go through the one provider-neutral client described above, with prompt caching on the Anthropic path.

The embeddings layer is either the Voyage AI API (production default, no local model) or a local nomic-embed-text model via sentence-transformers. Both are asymmetric document/query embedders.', "Section: WHAT THE PROJECT IS
The problem it solves: recruiters reading a CV cannot tell verified fact from self-promotion, cannot quickly gauge how strong a candidate actually is in a given skill, and cannot interrogate a static document. This system turns a pile of documents into an interactive, grounded, honest representative that cites real evidence, refuses to answer what it does not know, and quantifies skill levels with a trained model instead of keyword guessing.

The whole system can run on either a 100% local open-source stack or an all-API cloud stack, selected purely through configuration with no code changes. The cloud configuration is what makes it deployable as an always-on, secured, low-cost service; the local configuration is what made it possible to build, train, and evaluate everything for free on the developer's own hardware.", 'Section: PLUGGABLE PROVIDERS
The reranker layer is either the Voyage rerank API (production default), a local Qwen3-Reranker, or "none" to skip reranking. The Qwen3 reranker is notable: it is a causal language model, not a classification cross-encoder, and is scored — per its model card — by reading the probability of the "yes" token at the final position of a query/document relevance prompt, rather than with a naive classification head.

The local backends are constructed lazily, so importing the embedder or reranker modules in the production (API) configuration never imports PyTorch or transformers. This is the mechanism that keeps the serve image torch-free while preserving the original "built entirely from local open-source models" story.', 'Section: THE CORE DESIGN DECISION: BUILD VS SERVE
The serve phase is the hosted, always-on recruiter chat. It only reads the prebuilt artifacts and performs retrieval plus LLM calls. It deliberately contains no heavy local model, so the serve image is small (no PyTorch, no transformers, no Unstructured) and fits free or near-free hosting tiers. This separation is what makes the project simultaneously sophisticated to build and cheap and simple to host. The lean serve dependencies are listed in requirements.txt; the full build/eval/training stack is in requirements-dev.txt.', 'Section: GPU MEMORY MANAGEMENT DURING SCORING
When the local stack is used, the DeBERTa scorer and the local Ollama LLM contend for the same GPU. The DeBERTa model with disentangled attention is memory-heavy, and a resident Ollama model with multiple parallel slots can occupy most of the card. To run both on a modest GPU, the build phase time-slices the device: just before the scoring burst it asks the Ollama server to evict its model from VRAM, runs the scorer, and then unloads the scorer and clears the CUDA cache so Ollama can reload (honoring its parallelism) for the question-answering work. The batch size of the scorer is also tunable via an environment variable. None of this applies to the API stack, where no local model is resident.', "Section: DEPLOYMENT AND SECURITY
Several security properties are enforced by design. In production only the recruiter chat is served and the setup page is hidden, so recruiters can neither edit the profile nor trigger ingestion. The candidate's private data (the vector database and the structured profile) is kept out of any public repository. API keys live only in environment variables or managed secrets and are never committed. This very project-overview document is intentionally free of private information and secrets.

Running costs are minimal. The all-API serve path uses a fast, inexpensive Claude model for the agent and router, and a low-cost Voyage tier for embeddings and reranking — typically only a few dollars per month of light use — and the hosting itself can be free. The expensive work (training the scorer, generating synthetic data, parsing documents, running the full evaluation) all happens locally at build time and adds nothing to the hosting bill.", 'Section: DESIGN PRINCIPLES AND WHAT MAKES IT NOTABLE
The standout engineering choices are: a trained, evidence-grounded skill scorer instead of keyword guessing; an agent that routes between verified facts, the scorer, candidate document search, and project search with intelligent fallbacks; a hybrid, fused, re-ranked retrieval pipeline fronted by a broad/specific router and query expansion; a serious multi-dimensional evaluation suite, including the per-gate retrieval failure localization that pinpoints whether recall or re-ranking lost a chunk; a fully synthetic, validated training-data pipeline built because the needed labels did not exist; and a clean build-versus-serve split with three pluggable provider layers that lets one codebase run entirely on free local open-source models or deploy as a cheap, secured, always-on cloud service', "Section: THE EVALUATION SUITE
Router Accuracy: did the broad/specific router classify each query correctly against its labeled route?

Per-gate Retrieval Analysis: for specific document questions with known evidence, it localizes exactly where a relevant chunk was lost — at ingestion, at recall, or at re-ranking — using the pre-rerank fused pool the pipeline exposes.

Ingestion Quality: chunk statistics, coverage, and summary quality.

The harness seeds each candidate's data into a fresh collection, runs the pipeline over that candidate's questions, then scores every component on the combined result set and generates an HTML report (with companion JSON and CSV) showing both per-candidate and aggregate breakdowns. It supports checkpointing and resuming, so a long run can be interrupted and continued. Because the LLM judges can themselves run on a free local model, a full evaluation can cost nothing to run."]
response:
The system can indeed run entirely on free, local models without paid APIs. Here's how:

1. **Pluggable Providers**:  
   - **LLM Layer**: Uses a local Ollama model (e.g., `llama2`, `mistral`, etc.) instead of paid APIs like Anthropic.  
   - **Embeddings**: Supports local models like `nomic-embed-text` via `sentence-transformers`.  
   - **Reranking**: Optional local `Qwen3-Reranker` (no API dependency).  

2. **Build vs. Serve Separation**:  
   - The **serve** phase (recruiter chat) runs lightweight, with no resident heavy models (e.g., no PyTorch/transformers required). This enables free or low-cost hosting.  
   - The **build** phase (training, evaluation, document parsing) occurs locally, leveraging free open-source tools.  

3. **No API Dependencies**:  
   - The system’s architecture avoids paid APIs entirely by default. The cloud configuration (e.g., Claude, Voyage AI) is optional; local models are the primary path.  

4. **Cost Efficiency**:  
   - Running on local models eliminates monthly API costs. The "all-local" stack is designed for zero-cost development, testing, and evaluation.  

This flexibility allows the system to operate fully offline or on minimal-cost hosting while maintaining functionality.
reference: Yes. Every layer is pluggable: the LLM can be a local Ollama model, embeddings can be local nomic-embed, and the reranker can be a local Qwen3-Reranker — so the whole system can run on a 100% local open-source stack, or on an all-API cloud stack, purely by configuration.

user_input: How do you avoid making things up?
retrieved_contexts: ["The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered tool that transforms a candidate's documents into an interactive, evidence-based representative for recruiters, addressing the challenge of distinguishing verified facts from self-promotion in static CVs and quantifying skill proficiency. It employs an agentic ReAct-style tool-calling architecture combined with a multi-stage Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline to retrieve, contextualize, and generate responses grounded in the candidate's data. The standout feature is its fine-tuned NLP model for skill-proficiency scoring, which uses a trained machine-learning scorer to quantify expertise rather than relying on keyword matching. The system operates on a flexible tech stack, supporting either a 100% local open-source setup or an API-driven cloud deployment, with a key architectural split between a build phase (for document processing and scoring) and a serve phase (for real-time interaction). Evaluation is conducted via a rigorous multi-dimensional suite, and the cloud configuration enables low-cost, always-on deployment while the local setup facilitates development and training without infrastructure costs."]
response:
The Candidate AI Agent avoids fabricating information through three key mechanisms:  
1. **Document-Centric RAG Pipeline**: All responses are grounded in the candidate’s uploaded documents (CVs, projects, certifications, etc.), retrieved via semantic search and contextualized to ensure accuracy.  
2. **Skill-Proficiency Scoring Model**: A fine-tuned machine learning model quantifies skills based on evidence from the documents, avoiding assumptions or keyword-based guesswork.  
3. **Rigorous Evaluation Suite**: The system is validated through a multi-dimensional evaluation framework to ensure outputs align strictly with the candidate’s verified data.  

This approach ensures all claims are traceable to the candidate’s documented experience, eliminating unverified assertions.
reference: I answer only from grounded evidence — verified structured fields, the curated skill evidence, or retrieved document chunks — and the system prompt instructs me never to guess. If the information is not found I say so rather than inventing it, and RAGAS faithfulness is evaluated to check that answers stay grounded.

retrieved_contexts: ["The Candidate AI Agent is an AI-powered tool that transforms a candidate's documents into an interactive, evidence-based representative for recruiters, addressing the challenge of distinguishing verified facts from self-promotion in static CVs and quantifying skill proficiency.
